texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: New experimental results for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine are presented for piperazine molalities up to 4 mol/kg of water, temperatures between 313 and 393 K, and pressures up to about 9 MPa. Furthermore, the solubility of hydrogen sulfide was measured in about 2 m MDEA + 2 m piperazine aqueous solutions at 353 K up to about 6 MPa. Considering the new experimental data, a thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S-methyldiethanolamine-water has been extended allowing for the presence of piperazine.
texts.text: New experimental results for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine are presented for piperazine molalities up to 4 mol/kg of water, temperatures between 313 and 393 K, and pressures up to about 9 MPa. Furthermore, the solubility of hydrogen sulfide was measured in about 2 m MDEA + 2 m piperazine aqueous solutions at 353 K up to about 6 MPa. Considering the new experimental data, a thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S-methyldiethanolamine-water has been extended allowing for the presence of piperazine.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aqueous solutions of N -methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) are widely used for the sweetening of sour gases, particularly for the selective removal of hydrogen sulfide from gaseous effluents (e.g. natural gases) which also contain carbon dioxide. In such solutions the sour gases are partially converted to ionic species while MDEA is protonated. As the absorption of carbon dioxide is hindered by slow kinetics, it is possible to favor the absorption of hydrogen sulfide over that of carbon dioxide. Process modifications attempt to improve the selective absorption of hydrogen sulfide by adding modifiers (usually primary or secondary amines) to the aqueous MDEA solution. Piperazine (PIPH2) is a commonly used modifier.
texts.text: Aqueous solutions of N -methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) are widely used for the sweetening of sour gases, particularly for the selective removal of hydrogen sulfide from gaseous effluents (e.g. natural gases) which also contain carbon dioxide. In such solutions the sour gases are partially converted to ionic species while MDEA is protonated. As the absorption of carbon dioxide is hindered by slow kinetics, it is possible to favor the absorption of hydrogen sulfide over that of carbon dioxide. Process modifications attempt to improve the selective absorption of hydrogen sulfide by adding modifiers (usually primary or secondary amines) to the aqueous MDEA solution. Piperazine (PIPH2) is a commonly used modifier.

texts.label: text

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and piperazine at first requires reliable information on the solubility of the single gases in water and in the aqueous solutions of the single amines. In addition, reliable information is required on the equilibrium constants of the interesting chemical reactions. Piperazine is protonated and diprotonated in aqueous solutions in the presence of a sour gas. Furthermore, carbon dioxide and piperazine react in aqueous solutions to form (three different) carbamate species.
texts.text: and piperazine at first requires reliable information on the solubility of the single gases in water and in the aqueous solutions of the single amines. In addition, reliable information is required on the equilibrium constants of the interesting chemical reactions. Piperazine is protonated and diprotonated in aqueous solutions in the presence of a sour gas. Furthermore, carbon dioxide and piperazine react in aqueous solutions to form (three different) carbamate species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In previous work [1] the protonation of N -methyldiethanolamine in aqueous solution was investigated at temperatures from 278 to 368 K. Furthermore, the solubility of the single gases carbon dioxide and hydrogensulfide in aqueous MDEAsolutions was investigated in the temperature range from 313 to 413 K, for MDEA molalities up to 8 mol/kg of water [2,3], and pressures up to about 8 MPa. New experimental results are now presented for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine at temperatures between 313 and 393 K, for piperazine molalities up to 4 mol/kg of water, and pressures up to about 9 MPa.
texts.text: In previous work [1] the protonation of N -methyldiethanolamine in aqueous solution was investigated at temperatures from 278 to 368 K. Furthermore, the solubility of the single gases carbon dioxide and hydrogensulfide in aqueous MDEAsolutions was investigated in the temperature range from 313 to 413 K, for MDEA molalities up to 8 mol/kg of water [2,3], and pressures up to about 8 MPa. New experimental results are now presented for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine at temperatures between 313 and 393 K, for piperazine molalities up to 4 mol/kg of water, and pressures up to about 9 MPa.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Taking under consideration the new experimental data, a thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S + MDEA + water is extended allowing for the presence of piperazine.
texts.text: Taking under consideration the new experimental data, a thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S + MDEA + water is extended allowing for the presence of piperazine.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Adescription of the experimental equipment and procedure, as well as details on the chemicals usedwith the exception of piperazine-(manufacturers, purities, pretreatment) can be found in previous publications (cf. e.g. [4]). Piperazine (anhydrous, ≥ 99 mass%) was from Fluka Feinchemikalien GmbH (Neu-Ulm, Germany) and was degassed under vacuum.
texts.text: Adescription of the experimental equipment and procedure, as well as details on the chemicals usedwith the exception of piperazine-(manufacturers, purities, pretreatment) can be found in previous publications (cf. e.g. [4]). Piperazine (anhydrous, ≥ 99 mass%) was from Fluka Feinchemikalien GmbH (Neu-Ulm, Germany) and was degassed under vacuum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The aqueous solutions were prepared in a storage tank by dissolving known amounts of piperazine in pure water. The molality of piperazine in the aqueous solution was determined gravimetrically with a relative uncertainty smaller than ± 0.1%. The mass of the charged gas (up to about 5.7 g) is determined by weighing with an uncertainty of ± 0.008 g. The volume of the aqueous solvent needed to dissolve the gas is determined in each experiment. The mass of the solvent is calculated-with a relative uncertainty of maximum 0.7%-from its volume and known density (from separate measurements with a vibrating tube densimeter). Two pressure transducers (WIKA GmbH, Klingenberg, Germany) for pressures ranging to 4 and 10 MPa, respectively, were used to determine the solubility pressure. Before and after each series of measurements, the transducers were calibrated against a high precision pressure gauge (Desgranges & Huot, Aubervilliers, France). The maximum uncertainty in the pressure measurement is 0.1% of each transducer's maximum reading. The temperature is determined with two calibrated platinum resistance thermometers placed in the heating jacket of the cell with an uncertainty below ± 0.1 K.
texts.text: The aqueous solutions were prepared in a storage tank by dissolving known amounts of piperazine in pure water. The molality of piperazine in the aqueous solution was determined gravimetrically with a relative uncertainty smaller than ± 0.1%. The mass of the charged gas (up to about 5.7 g) is determined by weighing with an uncertainty of ± 0.008 g. The volume of the aqueous solvent needed to dissolve the gas is determined in each experiment. The mass of the solvent is calculated-with a relative uncertainty of maximum 0.7%-from its volume and known density (from separate measurements with a vibrating tube densimeter). Two pressure transducers (WIKA GmbH, Klingenberg, Germany) for pressures ranging to 4 and 10 MPa, respectively, were used to determine the solubility pressure. Before and after each series of measurements, the transducers were calibrated against a high precision pressure gauge (Desgranges & Huot, Aubervilliers, France). The maximum uncertainty in the pressure measurement is 0.1% of each transducer's maximum reading. The temperature is determined with two calibrated platinum resistance thermometers placed in the heating jacket of the cell with an uncertainty below ± 0.1 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility of hydrogen sulfide was measured in about 2 m (and 4 m) piperazine aqueous solutions at temperatures from 313 to 393 K in 20 K intervals (cf. Table 1). The experimental results for the total pressure above those solutions are plotted in Fig. 1 versus the stoichiometric molality of the gas. Throughout the paper, molality (i.e. number of moles/kg of water) is used for describing the solute concentration.

texts.orig: The behavior of the system H2S + piperazine + H2O is very similar to that observed for the system H2S + MDEA + H2O (cf. [3]). Adding a sour gas to a piperazine-containing aqueous solution at first only slightly increases the total pressure above the solution as the gas is mostly dissolved in non-volatile, ionic form. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Chemical equilibrium in the aqueous phase
texts.text: 3.1. Chemical equilibrium in the aqueous phase

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to chemical reactions in the liquid phase, hydrogen sulfide is dissolved in the liquid phase not only in neutral, but also in non-volatile, ionic form. The following chemical reactions are considered: the autoprotolysis of water (reaction R1), the formation and dissociation of bisulfide (reactions R2, R3), the protonation of methyldiethanolamine (reaction R4), and the protonation and diprotonation of piperazine (reactions R5, R6).
texts.text: Due to chemical reactions in the liquid phase, hydrogen sulfide is dissolved in the liquid phase not only in neutral, but also in non-volatile, ionic form. The following chemical reactions are considered: the autoprotolysis of water (reaction R1), the formation and dissociation of bisulfide (reactions R2, R3), the protonation of methyldiethanolamine (reaction R4), and the protonation and diprotonation of piperazine (reactions R5, R6).

texts.orig: Solving this set of equations for a given temperature and given stoichiometric mole numbers ¯ ni of mixed components (i.e. H2S, MDEA, piperazine, and H2O) results in the 'true' composition of the liquid phase, i.e. the molalities of all neutral and ionic species.
texts.text: Solving this set of equations for a given temperature and given stoichiometric mole numbers ¯ ni of mixed components (i.e. H2S, MDEA, piperazine, and H2O) results in the 'true' composition of the liquid phase, i.e. the molalities of all neutral and ionic species.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In principle, MDEA and piperazine might also be present in the vapor phase. However, as on one hand the vapor pressure of both amines is very small in the temperature range considered here (see e.g. [7,8]), and on the other hand, the molecular concentration of both amines in the liquid is very low in the high pressure region-which is of interest in the present work-, where they are (practically) completely protonized, their presence in the vapor phase is neglected.
texts.text: In principle, MDEA and piperazine might also be present in the vapor phase. However, as on one hand the vapor pressure of both amines is very small in the temperature range considered here (see e.g. [7,8]), and on the other hand, the molecular concentration of both amines in the liquid is very low in the high pressure region-which is of interest in the present work-, where they are (practically) completely protonized, their presence in the vapor phase is neglected.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3.1. Binary aqueous subsystems
texts.text: 3.3.1. Binary aqueous subsystems

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When one of the single components H2S, MDEA or piperazine is dissolved in pure water, with the exception of very dilute solutions chemical reactions can be neglected. Thus, from experimental results on the vapor-liquid equilibrium of an aqueous solution of the mentioned components, only interaction parameters between the single solutes (either H2S, MDEA or piperazine) can be determined.
texts.text: When one of the single components H2S, MDEA or piperazine is dissolved in pure water, with the exception of very dilute solutions chemical reactions can be neglected. Thus, from experimental results on the vapor-liquid equilibrium of an aqueous solution of the mentioned components, only interaction parameters between the single solutes (either H2S, MDEA or piperazine) can be determined.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the stoichiometric molality of the sour gas surmounts that of the base piperazine, the sour gas can no longer be absorbed chemically, i.e. in ionic form, but has to be dissolved physically, i.e. in neutral molecular form. As the aqueous solution becomes more and more acidic, the protonated piperazine is diprotonated. In that region, the liquid can be regarded as an aqueous mixture of two strong electrolytes (HSPIPH3 and (HS)2PIPH4), where H2S is physically dissolved. The solubility data for this system might therefore be described with interaction parameters for the binary subsystems H2S + H2O, HSPIPH3 + H2O, and (HS)2PIPH4 + H2O,andfortheternary subsystems H2S + HSPIPH3 + H2OandH2S + ( HS ) 2 PIPH4 + H2O. As mentioned above, interaction parameters are only available for the binary subsystem H2S + H2O. Interaction parameters for the binary subsystems HSPIPH3 + H2O and (HS)2PIPH4 + H2O, as well as for the ternary subsystems H2S + HSPIPH3 + H2O and H2S + ( HS ) 2 PIPH4 + H2O are not available. The new experimental data for the solubility of H2S in PIPH2 + H2O was therefore used to determine those parameters. The regions, where an additional liquid phase appeared, were excluded from the fitting runs. In the course of the fitting, parameters for the ternary subsystem H2S + ( HS ) 2PIPH4 + H2O turned out not to be of importance and were therefore set to zero. Altogether, nine additional interaction parameters had to be considered (cf. Table 4).
texts.text: the stoichiometric molality of the sour gas surmounts that of the base piperazine, the sour gas can no longer be absorbed chemically, i.e. in ionic form, but has to be dissolved physically, i.e. in neutral molecular form. As the aqueous solution becomes more and more acidic, the protonated piperazine is diprotonated. In that region, the liquid can be regarded as an aqueous mixture of two strong electrolytes (HSPIPH3 and (HS)2PIPH4), where H2S is physically dissolved. The solubility data for this system might therefore be described with interaction parameters for the binary subsystems H2S + H2O, HSPIPH3 + H2O, and (HS)2PIPH4 + H2O,andfortheternary subsystems H2S + HSPIPH3 + H2OandH2S + ( HS ) 2 PIPH4 + H2O. As mentioned above, interaction parameters are only available for the binary subsystem H2S + H2O. Interaction parameters for the binary subsystems HSPIPH3 + H2O and (HS)2PIPH4 + H2O, as well as for the ternary subsystems H2S + HSPIPH3 + H2O and H2S + ( HS ) 2 PIPH4 + H2O are not available. The new experimental data for the solubility of H2S in PIPH2 + H2O was therefore used to determine those parameters. The regions, where an additional liquid phase appeared, were excluded from the fitting runs. In the course of the fitting, parameters for the ternary subsystem H2S + ( HS ) 2PIPH4 + H2O turned out not to be of importance and were therefore set to zero. Altogether, nine additional interaction parameters had to be considered (cf. Table 4).

texts.orig: The experimental data for the total pressure above aqueous solutions of piperazine and hydrogen sulfide (with no additional liquid phase) are correlated with an average relative deviation of 2.6%. (2.3 and 3%, at 2 and 4 m piperazine, respectively). Fig. 1 shows a comparison between experimental and calculated total pressures (for preset temperature and stoichiometric molality of piperazine and the sour gas) above aqueous 2 and 4 m solutions of piperazine.
texts.text: The experimental data for the total pressure above aqueous solutions of piperazine and hydrogen sulfide (with no additional liquid phase) are correlated with an average relative deviation of 2.6%. (2.3 and 3%, at 2 and 4 m piperazine, respectively). Fig. 1 shows a comparison between experimental and calculated total pressures (for preset temperature and stoichiometric molality of piperazine and the sour gas) above aqueous 2 and 4 m solutions of piperazine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The appearance of a second liquid phase is not taken into account by the model. But, two approaches were made in order to consider the second liquid, 'hydrogen sulfide rich' phase. On the one hand, it was assumed that this phase appears, when the partial pressure of hydrogen sulfide equals its pure component vapor pressure. This follows from Raoult's law, applied to hydrogen sulfide, disregarding the Poynting correction and real gas effects, and assuming that the second liquid phase consists of pure hydrogen sulfide. On the other hand, the phase equilibrium between the coexisting liquid phases was expressed with distribution coefficients for both hydrogen sulfide and water (for details see e.g. [9]). Both approaches give good results only for temperatures up to 333 K. Dashed and full lines in Fig. 1 give calculated results from the first and second approach, respectively.
texts.text: The appearance of a second liquid phase is not taken into account by the model. But, two approaches were made in order to consider the second liquid, 'hydrogen sulfide rich' phase. On the one hand, it was assumed that this phase appears, when the partial pressure of hydrogen sulfide equals its pure component vapor pressure. This follows from Raoult's law, applied to hydrogen sulfide, disregarding the Poynting correction and real gas effects, and assuming that the second liquid phase consists of pure hydrogen sulfide. On the other hand, the phase equilibrium between the coexisting liquid phases was expressed with distribution coefficients for both hydrogen sulfide and water (for details see e.g. [9]). Both approaches give good results only for temperatures up to 333 K. Dashed and full lines in Fig. 1 give calculated results from the first and second approach, respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. System H 2 S + MDEA + piperazine + H 2 O
texts.text: 4.2. System H 2 S + MDEA + piperazine + H 2 O

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of both MDEA and piperazine can be predicted from the model described before. Predictions for the total pressure are compared to the new experimental results in Fig. 2. Experimental pressures are predicted with an average relative deviation of 6.4%. Those predictions are rather good, although parameters for some important interactions-e.g. parameters for interactions between species of MDEA on one side and species of piperazine on the other side, or ternary parameters between the sour gas, MDEA, and piperazine (and their species)-are neglected (cf. Fig. 5).
texts.text: The solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of both MDEA and piperazine can be predicted from the model described before. Predictions for the total pressure are compared to the new experimental results in Fig. 2. Experimental pressures are predicted with an average relative deviation of 6.4%. Those predictions are rather good, although parameters for some important interactions-e.g. parameters for interactions between species of MDEA on one side and species of piperazine on the other side, or ternary parameters between the sour gas, MDEA, and piperazine (and their species)-are neglected (cf. Fig. 5).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sour gas sweetening is often achieved by absorption in aqueous solutions of amines. The basic design of such absorption processes requires a thermodynamic model for the solubility of sour gases, e.g. carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide, in aqueous solutions of one or more amines, e.g. methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)and piperazine. The present work is an extension of previous work on the solubility of the single gases carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of MDEA [2,3]. New experimental results are presented for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine. The previously presented thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S + MDEA + H2O has been extended allowing for the presence of piperazine. New VLE data on the system H2S + MDEA + piperazine + H2O is used to assess model predictions.
texts.text: Sour gas sweetening is often achieved by absorption in aqueous solutions of amines. The basic design of such absorption processes requires a thermodynamic model for the solubility of sour gases, e.g. carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide, in aqueous solutions of one or more amines, e.g. methyldiethanolamine (MDEA)and piperazine. The present work is an extension of previous work on the solubility of the single gases carbon dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of MDEA [2,3]. New experimental results are presented for the solubility of hydrogen sulfide in aqueous solutions of piperazine. The previously presented thermodynamic model for describing phase equilibria for the system H2S + MDEA + H2O has been extended allowing for the presence of piperazine. New VLE data on the system H2S + MDEA + piperazine + H2O is used to assess model predictions.
